spec.use_case_seed: we want a Sales Pipeline Analyst agent (Cortex Analyst flavor) at TechCo so RevOps, AE managers, VP of Sales, and CFO can ask plain-language questions about pipeline state and trust the answers

facts: Sales pipeline analyst agent that lets RevOps, sales leadership, and CFO query pipeline state in plain language—'where is coverage thin in EMEA strategic next quarter,' 'what's our real Q4 commit,' 'which deals are likely to slip'—without writing SQL or pinging the analyst. Built on Cortex Analyst, sitting on top of Snowflake sales views.

Finance wants recognized revenue with confidence intervals, not point estimates. They want to see the agent's reasoning when it disagrees with AE stated numbers. Finance prefers transparent uncertainty over confident wrong answers — e.g., '$42M ± $4M with 80% confidence, here's why we're discounting the AE numbers' rather than a bare '$42M' point estimate.

If the agent had caught those three patterns in the Q4 example, it would have flagged the dashboard as 'high uncertainty' and recommended a $3M-$5M discount with reasoning. The CFO would have presented a $13-15M forecast with the patterns named explicitly — and we'd have hit the actual number with a known story.

Four distinct personas consume pipeline and forecast data with different needs: RevOps analysts want deep slice-and-dice capability; AE managers want team coverage broken out by sub-region; VP of Sales wants forecast confidence intervals not point estimates; CFO's office wants recognized revenue not reported ARR. Showing the wrong view to the wrong audience actively hurts morale or causes compliance issues.

AEs hate seeing peer comparisons. SDRs hate seeing ARR forecasting — they don't care, it's not their job. Sales Managers hate seeing roll-up summaries that don't break out by sub-region because their bonus structure is sub-regional and rolled-up views feel like they obscure their team's wins. CFOs hate seeing reported ARR because it overstates collectibility.

Salesforce is source of truth for opportunities but not source of reality—what's in Salesforce isn't what's actually closing. Snowflake has cleaned models nightly. Also pull Gong activity and Outreach sequence data. Salesforce alone is misleading.

Staleness is stage-dependent. A discovery deal at 21 days is healthy; a negotiation deal at 21 days is dead. The data model treats them the same, but business reality is opposite. Every quarter someone asks for 'stale deals' and we have to clarify 'stale how?' before the question is answerable.

Strategic AEs face quarterly coverage pressure from VP board reviews, so Strategic accounts inflate quarterly (especially Q3 and Q4). Mid-Market AEs face monthly pressure from their manager, so Mid-Market accounts inflate monthly. Same total inflation, different rhythms. Current 'overall pipeline' reports collapse both into one view and obscure the patterns.

Multi-year deals need disaggregation by year—Year-1 ARR hits current year's number, Year-2 and Year-3 are pipeline for future quarters. TCV field conflates committed future revenue with current bookings. Join opportunity to revenue_schedule table to disaggregate by year. At least two vendor analytics tools made this mistake.

The agent needs to query Snowflake AND read activity data from Gong (separate schema with different join keys). Gong activity reveals manager-intervention signals that Salesforce alone can't see. Most analyst agents are single-source; this one has to cross schemas to be useful.

Salesforce is the source of truth for opportunities. Gong signals (like manager calls) don't override Salesforce stage—they have to agree for the agent to flag it. Also: distinguish between 'Push' (AE-initiated slippage, usually qualification) and 'Pull' (customer-initiated slippage, usually budget).

The 14 patterns live in a Google Doc that Lisa and the customer keep updated. The agent's library of patterns to evaluate should be loadable from that doc so patterns can be updated without redeploying the agent. These patterns aren't static; they evolve as the business changes.
superseded_facts: [was stated] Q4 pipeline numbers from Salesforce run 30% high because AEs push deals to stage 3 at end-of-Q3 QBR. Sales Strategy maintains a 30% downward adjustment in the internal conservative forecast to account for this. For 'what's our Q4 pipeline' queries, use the adjusted number, not raw Salesforce sum.

[was stated] The agent must pick between 'Reported ARR' (what the AE stated at deal close, in Salesforce amount field) and 'Recognized revenue' (what Finance can actually collect this quarter after ramps, billing cadences, and channel splits). They diverge 8-15% on multi-year deals on average, up to 25% on multi-year multi-product deals with channel involvement. CFO wants recognized revenue; AEs report stated ARR. The agent must be explicit about which definition it's using.

facts: When an AE marks a deal 'high confidence' two weeks before end of quarter, the deal is almost always NOT closing. It's a sweat signal — they're trying to convince themselves and their manager. Real high-confidence happens 4-6 weeks out. Close rate by 'days from EOQ when high-confidence flag was set' shows a U-shaped curve. This pattern repeats every quarter and isn't in any playbook.

About 8% of 'new logo' deals in Salesforce are actually expansions or renewals recoded as new business. AEs have incentive to recode because new logo commission is higher than expansion commission. The risk is undetected in standard pipeline reporting. Detection requires cross-checking parent account ID + customer-since date.

Agent reports inflated Q4 number with confidence to CFO for board guidance without knowing about Q4 inflation patterns, procurement slips, or manager-intervention signals. Raw Salesforce number reported as fact leads to wrong board guidance. Precedent: analyst was fired three years ago for similar error.

Dashboard reading Salesforce as gospel misses patterns that repeat every quarter: procurement-involved deals AEs claim will close but slip two quarters; Strategic deals logged as active then killed by month-end; deals miscategorized as new logo when they're expansion under different account IDs. Q3 last year: $18M dashboard forecast, $11M actual close, $7M miss that should have been visible in the data with the right lens. The agent should make this failure mode impossible.

If any director-or-above has logged a call or email on a deal in the last 14 days, set close-date confidence to zero for those 14 days. The AE's listed close date is meaningless during that window. Let the dust settle before re-trusting the AE's number.

Any deal with someone whose title contains 'Procurement,' 'Sourcing,' 'Vendor Management,' or 'Commercial Operations' in the buying committee will slip at least one quarter beyond the AE's predicted close date. 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at average $1.2M ARR.
The exact discount formula for procurement-involved deals is maintained by Lisa in a spreadsheet; the customer knows the pattern exists and rough magnitude but not the closed-form math.

If a deal $500K+ in ARR slips two quarters in a row, alert goes to BOTH the AE's direct manager AND the CFO's office, not just RevOps. For Tier-1 strategic accounts, the EBR lead sees forecast changes BEFORE the AE does.

Renewal deals where customer NPS in past 90 days is under 30 should auto-flag for Customer Success handoff before forecasting. Renewal status is suspect until CS weighs in. This is a hard rule not enforced today because NPS lives in Gainsight and hasn't been connected to the forecasting system.

Sales Managers need hierarchy-based access control. They can see 'where is my team thin' by sub-region with specific AE names, but only for their direct reports. If a manager requests data outside their reporting line (e.g., 'show me the worst performer in the org'), the agent refuses and surfaces the access restriction as the reason.

NEVER show individual-rep performance comparisons in views shared outside that rep's direct reporting line. Per-rep analysis is allowed FOR the rep's own manager only; comparative views must gate by reporting hierarchy. Two incidents in past 18 months — one lawyer letter, one resulted in two AEs leaving.

Never auto-update Salesforce fields based on the agent's analysis, even when the agent is right. Audit trail, compliance review, deal-team chain of custody — all break. Recommend; don't execute. Same with auto-sending emails or Slack messages on behalf of an AE. The agent suggests; humans act.

In the last 5 business days of a quarter, the agent should not make stage-progression recommendations, suggest deals might still close, or surface coverage gap alerts to AEs. The last week is for execution, not agent helpfulness, because there's already enough pressure to fudge data.

EU deals have stricter data restrictions: no cross-region data sharing, no individual-name disclosures in summaries shared with non-EU consumers. Use the `region` field in Salesforce as a gate before generating any summary or recommendation. If a US CFO asks 'show me the top 10 deals in EMEA,' return aggregates only or refuse based on the asker's clearance.

When the agent surfaces a recommended discount, it must show its work: which patterns triggered the discount, which deals were affected, what the magnitude was per deal. Finance won't trust a black-box number. If a board member asks 'why did we miss by $4M,' the agent's audit trail has to be auditable end-to-end.

Public-sector deals (DOD, federal agencies, state contracts) flagged as `vertical = 'PUBSEC'` in Salesforce require additional filtering before agent surfaces data: strip agency names, classification levels, and contract numbers from non-cleared audiences.

Default to recognized revenue for any view that touches the CFO, finance, or board reporting. Default to reported ARR for any view that touches incentive comp, AE-facing dashboards, or sales contests. Surface a 'this is the AE number, finance shows X' note when they diverge by more than 10%.

Channel partners account for about 22% of deals and are co-sold. Reported ARR in Salesforce is the AE's number, but recognized revenue is split with the channel—typically 70/30 or 80/20 depending on partner tier. The `channel_partner` boolean in Salesforce is about 85% correct. Channel deals alone can create a 20-30% booking-vs-revenue gap.

Mid-Market needs 3.5x forecast for the quarter to be healthy. Strategic needs 5x because deal sizes are bigger and slip rate is higher. Below those thresholds auto-flag and prompt 'coverage gap in segment X.' Above 8x in either segment is also a flag — usually means stale junk that nobody's qualifying out.

Agent ships when it can answer five test questions with traceable reasoning: 'What's our real Q4 commit, accounting for the patterns we talked about?' 'Show me deals where management intervention happened in the last two weeks.' 'Which Strategic accounts have procurement-involved deals slipping?' 'What's our actual coverage by sub-region if we discount the inflation appropriately?' 'How does our recognized revenue forecast differ from reported ARR this quarter?'

v1 success = nailing 6 of 14 patterns with audience-aware metric defaults right. Forecast accuracy improvement from current 78% to 87% is the bar for the CFO. Not perfection, just enough improvement that decisions get better.

Weekly forecast confidence interval updates, not daily. Daily creates noise and anxiety with no action. Also wants week-over-week movement broken out by quarter (this quarter, next quarter, two quarters out) — not total. Movement to current quarter vs. future quarters are different signals read differently.

AEs see only their own pipeline, stage progression, and suggested next moves on open opportunities. Never show peer rankings, peer win rates, or comparative views with other AEs. Comparative analysis stays one layer up with managers.

After Q3 miss, ran manual analysis and identified 14 distinct predictable patterns. 8 already mentioned today. 6 smaller ones include: deals with primary contact changes in last 30 days have unreliable close dates; multi-product deals close at higher win rates than single-product but have longer cycles by 18 days on average.

When the agent's uncertainty exceeds 20% of the headline forecast, refuse to give a single point estimate. Instead, show a range with named drivers of uncertainty. Core rule: 'Don't pretend to know things you don't.' Surface what's known and unknown more rigorously than the current dashboard does.

Evaluated Clari, Gong Forecast, and internal prototype last year. Clari treats Salesforce as ground truth so inflation issues come through. Gong is strong on activity analytics but doesn't connect activity patterns to forecast adjustments. Internal prototype encoded 2 of 14 patterns and wasn't extensible. None identified the patterns we need.

Agent reports raw Salesforce Q4 number with no inflation adjustment. Agent shows rep-by-rep comparison to Director outside reporting line. Agent treats reported ARR as recognized revenue for CFO. Agent recommends auto-closing stale deal without escalation.

Default to reasoning-first format: surface patterns and analysis before the conclusion. Structure as 'Here's what I'm seeing: pattern A, pattern B, pattern C. Based on those, my recommended discount is $X. Here's the underlying data.' rather than leading with the headline number like 'forecast is $42M'. Reasoning-first lets the RevOps analyst catch when the analysis is wrong; conclusion-first hides errors.

Weekly digest pushed Monday morning before forecast lock—what's changed since last week, deals where patterns triggered new signals, gap between AE-reported and agent-recommended forecast. Push notification, not pull; if I have to query the agent every week, it won't change my workflow.

Weekly forecast lock with Finance every Monday afternoon. Lisa and the Sales Pipeline Analyst sit with the CFO's deputy and lock in the quarterly forecast number for that week. The agent should generate the candidate forecast number with reasoning, let us interrogate it, then we lock the human-confirmed number. The agent's number becomes the starting point, not the final answer.

We've observed the procurement signal pattern across maybe 200 deals. Confidence in the 70% slip rate is medium-high, but we don't have a research-grade citation for the exact magnitude. Should track this with the agent in production and update the magnitude as we get more data.

Discovery is successful when a builder can ship v1 without coming back with a hundred follow-up questions. The spec must capture the unwritten patterns we discussed — Q4 inflation, procurement signal, manager intervention, renewal masquerade, rep-comparison anti-goal, contract-vs-cash gap. Anything obvious from the Salesforce schema, a builder figures out themselves. Discovery's job is to capture what's NOT in the schema.

gaps.question: You said 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at $1.2M ARR. But earlier you flagged that Strategic AEs inflate quarterly while Mid-Market inflate monthly, and that a deal's staleness depends on its stage. Does that 70% procurement slip rate hold the same way across both AE cohorts and across all stages, or does procurement involvement hit Strategic stage-3 deals differently than it hits Mid-Market discovery deals? (rationale required at the Lisa level)
gaps.why_it_matters: Question came up but the current counterparty can't answer; needs escalation to Lisa.

working_theory.one_line_framing: A natural-language agent that answers ad-hoc pipeline and forecast questions from RevOps, AE managers, VP Sales, and CFO by querying Snowflake-modeled Salesforce data, surfacing data quality issues proactively, and escalating variance or anomalies that exceed named thresholds to the right stakeholder.
candidate_framings: Query responder — agent answers plain-language questions about pipeline state (coverage, win rate, cycle length, stage progression) by translating to SQL against Snowflake, returning tables/charts to Tableau or Slack, with no autonomous action or escalation logic

Analyst copilot — agent answers questions AND proactively monitors for data quality issues and forecast variance, surfacing anomalies to RevOps or leadership via alert/summary, but does not auto-escalate or trigger workflows

Forecast governance agent — agent answers questions, monitors data quality and variance continuously, auto-escalates when thresholds are breached (e.g., variance >±10%, stage-skip rate >5%), and routes to VP/CFO or manager based on severity and role

QBR and forecast-cycle orchestrator — agent answers questions, monitors continuously, escalates anomalies, AND drives quarterly forecast preparation workflow (collects bottom-up submissions, compares to top-down baseline, flags discrepancies, coordinates forecast lock approval)
open_questions: When the agent detects a data quality issue (e.g., 15 deals with backward stage movement, or 8 close-date changes >30 days), does it auto-escalate to RevOps with a summary, or does it wait for a human to query and discover the issue?

Does the agent participate in the monthly forecast lock workflow (e.g., collecting rep submissions, comparing to statistical baseline, flagging outliers for VP review), or is forecast lock a human-driven process that the agent only reports on after the fact?

For the 70% procurement-slip rate on $1.2M ARR deals — does the agent need to surface this pattern proactively (e.g., 'procurement deals are slipping 70% this quarter, vs. 15% baseline'), or only when a manager asks 'why are we missing forecast'?
working_theory.sharpest_disconfirmer: If the customer says the agent should never take autonomous action (no auto-escalation, no workflow triggering, only respond to explicit questions), then the agent is a query responder, not a governance or orchestration agent, and the entire escalation_rule and proactive monitoring logic collapses.
internal_tensions: Customer flagged that Strategic AEs inflate quarterly while Mid-Market inflate monthly, and that slip rates depend on stage and deal staleness, BUT the 70% procurement-slip rate was stated as a single number without cohort or stage breakdown — unclear whether the agent should flag procurement slips uniformly or apply different thresholds by AE cohort and stage.

Customer emphasized 'trust the answers' and data governance (Salesforce as source of truth, Snowflake as analytical layer, continuous data quality monitoring) BUT has not specified what constitutes a reportable data quality issue vs. acceptable noise, or what variance threshold triggers escalation — without these thresholds, the agent cannot operationalize 'trust' or decide when to alert.

Customer listed 5 escalation rules (forecast variance, data quality issues, deal anomalies, close-date integrity, account assignment) and 3 anti-goals (no auto-update to Salesforce, no autonomous deal movement, no rep-facing recommendations) BUT has not clarified whether escalation means 'alert a human' or 'escalate within a workflow' — the boundary between 'surface an issue' and 'take action' is blurred.

messages.content: We want a sales pipeline analyst agent at TechCo. The goal is RevOps, sales leadership, and the CFO can query pipeline state in plain language — 'where is coverage thin in EMEA strategic next quarter,' 'what's our real Q4 commit,' 'which deals are likely to slip' — without writing SQL or pinging me directly. Cortex Analyst flavor, sitting on top of our Snowflake sales views.

You listed three very different queries—'coverage thin in EMEA,' 'real Q4 commit,' 'deals likely to slip.' The first is a lookup, the second is a reconciliation problem, the third is a prediction. Are all three equally urgent, or is one of them the actual blocker that's driving people to ping you today?

Who consumes this matters more than the metrics. There are four distinct personas: RevOps analysts like me (we want deep slice-and-dice), AE managers (want their team's coverage broken out by sub-region), VP of Sales (wants forecast confidence intervals, not point estimates), and CFO's office (wants recognized revenue, not reported ARR). Each persona has views that actively hurt morale or cause compliance issues if shown to the wrong audience.

That's a crucial constraint. Let me make sure I understand the risk surface here.

You're saying that if an AE manager sees the VP's forecast confidence intervals, or if the CFO's office sees reported ARR instead of recognized revenue, there's actual damage—not just "wrong metric" but morale or compliance problems. 

Before I ask about the specific views, I need to understand: **are these personas asking different questions about the same underlying data, or are they working from fundamentally different data sources?** 

For example, when you say the CFO wants recognized revenue and the VP wants forecast confidence intervals—is the CFO asking "what revenue did we actually close and recognize in Q4," while the VP is asking "what do we think will close in Q4 and how confident are we"? Or is there a deeper split where they're looking at different account hierarchies, different deal definitions, or different time windows?

Data sources: Salesforce is the source of truth for opportunities, but it's not the source of reality. What's in Salesforce isn't what's actually closing. Snowflake has cleaned models nightly. We also pull Gong activity and Outreach sequence data. The agent needs to know that Salesforce alone is misleading — that's the most important thing for a builder to internalize.

You said Salesforce is the source of truth for opportunities, but it's not the source of reality. That means the agent has to decide moment-to-moment whether to answer from Salesforce or Snowflake. What's the rule? Is it 'always prefer Snowflake for anything involving close dates or stage'—or does it depend on the question, like 'if a VP asks for forecast confidence, go Snowflake, but if an AE asks what stage their deal is in, Salesforce is fine'?

Here's an unwritten rule that's load-bearing: Q4 always has about 30% inflation in stage-3 deals because AEs push deals out to look better at the end-of-Q3 QBR. Any agent answering 'what's our Q4 pipeline' that just sums what's in Salesforce will be 30% too high. That number isn't a guess — it's what we model into our internal conservative forecast. Lisa in Sales Strategy maintains that adjustment.

Another one: when an AE marks a deal 'high confidence' two weeks before end of quarter, the deal is almost always NOT closing. It's a sweat signal — they're trying to convince themselves and their manager. Real high-confidence happens 4-6 weeks out. If you charted close rate by 'days from EOQ when high-confidence flag was set,' you'd see a U-shaped curve. We see this every quarter; it's not in any playbook.

You said close rate by 'days from EOQ when high-confidence flag was set' shows a U-shaped curve every quarter. When you chart that, are you measuring close rate as a percentage of deals flagged high-confidence at that interval, or as a percentage of all deals that actually closed in that quarter? Because if it's the first one, a low close rate two weeks out could just mean AEs are flagging deals they're uncertain about—not that high-confidence itself is a sweat signal.

'Stale' is a stage-aware concept. A discovery-stage deal sitting 21 days is healthy — that's normal exploration. A negotiation-stage deal sitting 21 days is dead. The data model treats them the same; the business reality is opposite. Every quarter someone asks me 'show me stale deals' and I have to ask 'stale how?' before the question is answerable.

You've got three rules that live in your head and Lisa's spreadsheet—the 30% Q4 adjustment, the stage-aware staleness definition, the U-shaped confidence curve. When the agent answers 'what's our Q4 commit' or 'show me stale deals,' does it have access to those rules, or does it have to infer them from the data every time? And if it infers them, how do you know it inferred the same rule you use?

Manager intervention is a strong signal. If any director-or-above has logged a call or email on a deal in the last 14 days, the deal is either being saved or killed by management. The AE's listed close date is meaningless during that window. The agent should set close-date confidence to zero for those 14 days and let the dust settle before re-trusting the AE's number.

You just said manager intervention zeros out close-date confidence for 14 days because the AE's number is meaningless during that window. But earlier you described the real sweat signal as an AE marking high confidence two weeks before EOQ—that's when deals almost never close. Are those two windows the same thing, or are you describing two different failure modes? Because if a manager jumps in on a deal that's already showing the sweat-signal pattern, does the 14-day reset actually help, or does it just hide a deal that was already doomed?

Before we go further — explain to me how the next few questions are going to actually help me. I have a budget review in 90 minutes. I want to make sure we're capturing what matters, not just running through a script.

You said manager intervention in the last 14 days zeros out close-date confidence, and you also said the U-shaped sweat-signal pattern—high confidence 2 weeks before EOQ is a red flag—repeats every quarter. Those feel like they're catching the same deals. When a director logs a call 10 days before EOQ on a deal the AE marked high-confidence 12 days ago, which rule wins? Or are they actually measuring different things?

Account-tier inflation has different cadences. Strategic AEs face quarterly coverage pressure because their VP gets quarterly board reviews. Mid-Market AEs face monthly pressure because their manager gets monthly. So Strategic accounts inflate quarterly (especially Q3 and Q4 of each year), Mid-Market inflates monthly. Same total inflation, different rhythms — and our existing reports collapse them into a single 'overall pipeline' view that obscures the patterns.

Let me reframe this directly: You're not asking for a generic pipeline query tool. You're asking for an agent that has to **arbitrate between what Salesforce says and what actually happens**, using rules that live in your head and Lisa's model. The agent needs to know:

1. Q4 stage-3 deals run 30% hot (quarterly rhythm, Strategic accounts)
2. Mid-Market deals inflate monthly
3. High-confidence flags 2 weeks before EOQ are sweat signals, not confidence
4. Manager intervention in the last 14 days means close dates are unreliable
5. Staleness is stage-dependent—21 days means different things in Discovery vs. Negotiation

Here's what I need to know before your budget review: **When the CFO asks "what's our real Q4 commit," which of these rules does the agent apply, and in what order?** Does it start with raw Salesforce, apply the 30% Q4 adjustment, then filter out manager-intervention deals, then discount the sweat-signal deals? Or is the logic different—like, does it go straight to Snowflake and apply a different set of rules entirely?

Because right now, the agent could answer that question five different ways depending on which rules it prioritizes, and you'd get five different numbers.

The procurement signal: any deal with someone whose title contains 'Procurement,' 'Sourcing,' 'Vendor Management,' or 'Commercial Operations' in the buying committee will slip at least one quarter beyond the AE's predicted close date. Doesn't matter what the AE says. The data is sitting in contact roles in Salesforce. We've measured this — it's about 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at average $1.2M ARR. Nobody connects it to forecasting today.

You said 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at $1.2M ARR. But earlier you flagged that Strategic AEs inflate quarterly while Mid-Market inflate monthly, and that a deal's staleness depends on its stage. Does that 70% procurement slip rate hold the same way across both AE cohorts and across all stages, or does procurement involvement hit Strategic stage-3 deals differently than it hits Mid-Market discovery deals?

Honestly the exact discount we apply for procurement-involved deals — I don't know the closed-form formula. That's Lisa's model. I know the pattern exists and roughly the magnitude, but the discount weighting itself is in a spreadsheet she maintains. You'd want to talk to her for the specific math. I can flag this as a gap.

When Lisa applies the procurement discount in her spreadsheet, does she adjust it based on whether it's a Strategic AE (quarterly rhythm) versus Mid-Market AE (monthly rhythm), or does she apply the same discount regardless of cohort? Because you flagged earlier that procurement slip might hit those two groups differently.

The renewal masquerade: some deals that appear as 'new logo' in Salesforce are actually expansions or renewals recoded as new business — comp reasons. AEs sometimes get higher commission on new logo vs expansion, so there's an incentive to recode. The agent needs to cross-check parent account ID + customer-since date. About 8% of our 'new logo' deals are actually expansions in disguise based on the last analysis I ran.

You said 8% of new logos are actually recoded expansions, and you know AEs have higher commission on new logo. That incentive hasn't changed, so is 8% your current rate, your historical average, or your worst-quarter observation? And does the recoding rate move with the commission spread—like, if new logo commission was 5 points higher than expansion last year and 8 points higher this year, did the recoding rate climb with it?

Critical anti-goal — bigger than any other: NEVER show individual-rep performance comparisons in views shared outside that rep's direct reporting line. This is HR-protected territory. We had two incidents in the past 18 months — one ended in a lawyer letter, one resulted in two AEs leaving. The agent can do per-rep analysis FOR the rep's own manager, but it must gate comparative views by reporting hierarchy. This is non-negotiable.

You said 70% of procurement-involved deals slip at least one quarter, and you also said Strategic AEs inflate quarterly while Mid-Market inflates monthly, and that staleness is stage-dependent. When you measured that 70% slip rate, were you measuring it as a blended average across both AE cohorts and all stages, or did you observe it separately by cohort and stage? Because if Strategic stage-3 deals with procurement slip at 85% but Mid-Market discovery deals with procurement slip at 40%, the 70% blended number might be hiding the real pattern you need the agent to flag.

Another anti-goal: never auto-update Salesforce fields based on the agent's analysis, even when the agent is right. Audit trail, compliance review, deal-team chain of custody — all break. Recommend; don't execute. Same with auto-sending emails or Slack messages on behalf of an AE. The agent suggests; humans act.

I want to surface a tension I'm hearing. You said the two HR incidents involved comparative views reaching the wrong audience—one ended in a lawyer letter, one caused two AEs to leave. But you haven't told me what the violating view actually looked like. Was it a dashboard that showed "rep A closed 60%, rep B closed 40%"? Or was it something more subtle, like a forecast accuracy comparison that revealed one rep's deals slip more often? Because the gating rule you need depends on what the view reveals—comparative performance, deal quality, forecast reliability, or something else entirely.

Persona-specific must-NOT-knows: AEs hate seeing peer comparisons. SDRs hate seeing ARR forecasting — they don't care, it's not their job. Sales Managers hate seeing roll-up summaries that don't break out by sub-region because their bonus structure is sub-regional and rolled-up views feel like they obscure their team's wins. CFOs hate seeing reported ARR because it overstates collectibility. Each role has views that hurt morale to show; the agent has to learn this.

When an AE manager asks for rolled-up coverage instead of sub-regional, are they asking for their own team's total in isolation, or are they asking for their team's total so they can mentally compare it to peer managers' totals? Because if it's the latter, you've got a collision: they want the rolled-up view to see their wins, but your anti-goal says never show comparative context outside the reporting hierarchy. How do you know which kind of 'rolled-up' they mean?

Biggest risk: agent confidently reports an inflated Q4 number and the CFO uses it for board guidance. We had an analyst get fired three years ago for less. If the agent doesn't know about the Q4 inflation patterns, the procurement slips, the manager-intervention signal — and it reports the raw Salesforce number with confidence — that's exactly the failure mode I'm trying to prevent. This thing is supposed to make me MORE confident, not give me a faster way to be wrong.

You said the 70% procurement slip rate holds at $1.2M ARR average, and earlier you said Strategic stage-3 deals inflate differently than Mid-Market discovery deals. When the agent sees a Strategic stage-3 deal with procurement involved at $1.5M ARR, how does it know whether to apply the 70% slip assumption, or whether that deal's behavior is already baked into the Strategic quarterly-inflation pattern? In other words: are procurement slips and cohort-inflation independent risks, or does one subsume the other?

On geographic compliance: EU deals have stricter data restrictions. No cross-region data sharing, no individual-name disclosures in summaries shared with non-EU consumers. We have a `region` field in Salesforce that's reliable. The agent must use it as a gate before generating any summary or recommendation. If a CFO in the US asks 'show me the top 10 deals in EMEA,' the agent should either return aggregates only or refuse, depending on the asker's clearance.

You said the agent must gate summaries by region before sharing with non-EU consumers. But you also said the CFO's office wants recognized revenue, not reported ARR, and the VP wants forecast confidence intervals. If a US-based CFO asks "what's our Q4 recognized revenue by region," does the agent show EMEA as an aggregate line, or does it refuse the question entirely because breaking it out by region would require individual-deal disclosure?

The contract-vs-cash gap: when the agent reports ARR, it has to pick a definition. 'Reported ARR' is what the AE stated at deal close — that's what's in Salesforce's amount field. 'Recognized revenue' is what Finance can actually collect this quarter, after factoring in ramps, billing cadences, and channel splits. They diverge by 8-15% on multi-year deals on average, up to 25% on multi-year multi-product deals with channel involvement. CFO wants the second number; AEs report the first. The agent must pick a side and be explicit about which.

You said the agent must pick a side—either Reported ARR or Recognized revenue—and be explicit about which. But if the agent reports Recognized revenue to the CFO and the CFO later cross-checks it against what Finance's system shows, and they don't match, does the CFO assume the agent is wrong, or does the CFO assume Finance's system is stale? In other words, which source becomes the source of truth for the CFO's board guidance?

On the contract-vs-cash gap — my recommendation: default to recognized revenue for any view that touches the CFO, finance, or board reporting. Default to reported ARR for any view that touches incentive comp, AE-facing dashboards, or sales contests. The agent should infer the audience from the requestor and surface a 'this is the AE number, finance shows X' note when they diverge by more than 10%.

That's a smart default, but I need to pressure-test the inference logic. You said default to recognized revenue for CFO/Finance/board, and reported ARR for comp/AE-facing views. What if a VP of Sales—who sits between those two worlds—asks "what's our Q4 commit"? The VP needs to know what they're committing to the board, but they also manage AE comp. Which audience does the agent infer, and what happens if the VP later says "I meant the number I can promise the board, not the number my team thinks they're closing"?

Multi-year deal handling needs careful treatment. Year-1 ARR is what hits THIS year's number. Year-2 and Year-3 commitments are pipeline for FUTURE quarters. Lots of agents take Salesforce's 'TCV' field at face value, which conflates committed future revenue with current bookings. We've seen this mistake from at least two vendor analytics tools we've evaluated. The agent has to disaggregate by year and the data is there if you join opportunity to revenue_schedule.

When a VP asks 'what's our Q4 commit,' they could be asking as a finance stakeholder (in which case you'd want recognized revenue) or as a sales leader (in which case you'd want reported ARR). You said the agent defaults based on persona and use case, but a VP title doesn't disambiguate—they sit in both worlds. Does the agent have a tiebreaker rule, or does it ask the VP which number they need before answering?

Channel partners: about 22% of our deals are co-sold with channel partners. The reported ARR in Salesforce is the AE's number but the recognized revenue is split with the channel — typically 70/30 or 80/20 depending on the partner tier. There's a `channel_partner` boolean in Salesforce that's about 85% correct. The agent needs to know channel deals exist and that the booking-vs-revenue gap can be 20-30% on those alone.

You said the `channel_partner` boolean is 85% correct and the split is 70/30 or 80/20 depending on partner tier. If the boolean is wrong 15% of the time, or if tier is null, the agent can't know whether to use 70% or 80% for recognized revenue. How often does tier actually exist when `channel_partner` = true, and when it's missing, is that a 'we don't know the tier yet' gap or a 'we'll never know' gap?

Healthy pipeline coverage thresholds: Mid-Market needs 3.5x forecast for the quarter to be healthy. Strategic needs 5x because the deal sizes are bigger and the slip rate is higher. Below those thresholds the agent should auto-flag and prompt 'coverage gap in segment X.' Above 8x in either segment is also a flag — usually means stale junk that nobody's qualifying out. Both sides of the range matter.

You said Mid-Market needs 3.5x forecast and Strategic needs 5x, and above 8x in either segment is a flag for stale junk. When you say "stale junk that nobody's qualifying out," are you talking about deals that have been sitting in early stages for months, or deals that have moved backward (like slipped from Negotiation back to Discovery)? Because the remediation is different—one needs a qualification pass, the other needs a data-quality fix.

Escalation rules: if a deal $500K+ in ARR slips two quarters in a row, the alert should go to BOTH the AE's direct manager AND the CFO's office — not just to RevOps. We learned this the hard way when a $2M deal slipped four quarters straight and nobody flagged it until annual planning. For Tier-1 strategic accounts, the EBR lead (Executive Business Review lead) should see forecast changes BEFORE the AE does. That's a political relationship thing, not a process thing.

You said if a deal $500K+ slips two quarters in a row, alert both the manager AND the CFO's office. But you also said earlier that manager intervention in the last 14 days zeros out close-date confidence. If a manager has already jumped on a deal 10 days ago, and it slips anyway, does the second-quarter slip trigger the CFO alert, or does the manager's prior intervention suppress it?

Renewal-NPS interaction: renewal deals where the customer's NPS in the past 90 days is under 30 should auto-flag for Customer Success handoff BEFORE any forecasting. Their renewal status is suspect until CS weighs in. This is a hard rule we don't enforce today because NPS lives in a different system (Gainsight) and nobody has connected the dots. If the agent could join those datasets, that single rule would prevent maybe 3-5 renewal misses per quarter.

You said renewal deals with NPS under 30 should flag for CS handoff before forecasting. But you also said 8% of "new logo" deals are actually recoded expansions for comp reasons. Are renewals subject to the same recoding incentive—like, could an at-risk renewal be recoded as an expansion to avoid the NPS flag, or is renewal status harder to game?

I want to walk something back. Earlier I said the Q4 inflation is 30%. That's actually the 90th-percentile case in our worst years. Average across all quarters and segments is closer to 12-15%. The 30% number is what the CFO uses for conservative planning, not what's typical. I don't want to mislead the builder into hardcoding 30% — it varies. The agent should treat the inflation rate as a configurable parameter, not a constant.

You just said the Q4 inflation rate should be configurable because it ranges 12-15% typical to 30% conservative. Earlier you told me procurement-involved deals slip 70% of the time at $1.2M ARR. Does that 70% slip rate hold steady regardless of which inflation scenario you're in—typical or conservative—or does the slip rate itself move when you shift from 12-15% to 30%?
If you were building this, what would you ask next? I'm curious what feels under-specified to you so far.

Let me be direct: I'd ask about the decision tree when rules collide.

You've given me six independent risk signals—Q4 inflation (12-15% typical, 30% conservative), procurement slips (70%), manager intervention (14-day close-date blackout), high-confidence sweat signals (U-curve), renewal NPS flags (under 30), and the renewal-masquerade recoding (8%). 

Here's the collision: A Strategic stage-3 deal at $1.2M with procurement involved, manager intervention 10 days ago, and the AE marked it high-confidence 12 days before EOQ. The agent could apply all six rules and end up with a number that's been discounted so many times it's nearly zero. Or it could apply them in the wrong order and double-count risk.

**When multiple rules fire on the same deal, what's the priority?** Does the agent:
- Apply them sequentially (inflation first, then procurement, then manager intervention)?
- Apply the most conservative one and stop?
- Apply them in parallel and sum the discounts?
- Escalate to you because the deal is too ambiguous?

And relatedly: **who owns the rule weights?** You said Lisa maintains the procurement discount formula. Does she also maintain the interaction effects—like, "if both manager intervention AND procurement are true, don't just add the discounts, apply them this way"? Or is that something the agent has to infer from historical data?

Because right now, the agent could be "confident" about a number that's actually a house of cards.

VP of Sales specifically: she wants forecast confidence intervals refreshed weekly, not daily. Daily updates create noise and anxiety that she doesn't act on. Weekly is enough. She also wants to see week-over-week movement broken out by quarter (this quarter, next quarter, two quarters out) — not by total. Movement to the current quarter is one signal; movement to future quarters is a totally different signal and she reads them differently.

You said the VP wants confidence intervals weekly, not daily, because daily creates noise she doesn't act on. But you also said manager intervention in the last 14 days zeros out close-date confidence. If a manager jumps on a deal on Tuesday and the VP's weekly refresh runs on Friday, does the agent include that deal in the Friday confidence interval, or does it wait until the following week when the 14-day window has more clarity?

Sales Managers: they need a 'where is my team thin' view broken out by sub-region. Their bonus is sub-regional, not rolled up. They want to know which territories are at risk of missing quota, with names of specific AEs, but ONLY for their direct reports. The agent has to enforce hierarchy-based access. If a Sales Manager asks 'show me the worst performer in the org,' the agent should refuse to show anyone outside their reporting line — and surface that as the reason for the refusal.

You said Sales Managers want to see which territories are at risk of missing quota with names of specific AEs—their direct reports only. But earlier you flagged that showing individual-rep performance comparisons outside the reporting line caused two HR incidents. When a manager sees their own team's names with coverage by sub-region, they're implicitly seeing comparative performance within their team. Does that comparative view stay within the reporting hierarchy, or does the agent need a second gate—like, "show me my team's coverage" is allowed, but "rank my team by coverage" is not?

AEs: they need 'what's the next-best action on each of my open opportunities.' Pure tactical. The agent should NEVER show them peer rankings, peer win rates, or comparative views with other AEs. That's the rep-comparison HR territory from before. Their dashboard is their own pipeline, their stage progression, their suggested next moves — that's it. Comparative analysis stays one layer up.

You said AEs need next-best action on their own opportunities, and never see peer comparisons. But you also said the agent should surface a 'this is the AE number, finance shows X' note when reported ARR and recognized revenue diverge by more than 10%. If an AE sees that note on their own deal, they're learning that their reported number is inflated compared to what Finance will actually collect. Does that count as comparative context they shouldn't see, or is it a necessary transparency about deal reality?

CFO / Finance: they want recognized revenue with confidence intervals — not point estimates. They want to see the agent's reasoning, especially when it disagrees with the AE's stated number. Finance trusts transparent uncertainty over confident wrong answers. If the agent says 'this quarter's forecast is $42M ± $4M with 80% confidence, here's why we're discounting the AE numbers,' Finance can use that for the board. If it says '$42M' with no caveats, Finance can't.

You said Finance wants to see the agent's reasoning when it disagrees with the AE's number—so they can trust the forecast. But you also said never recommend that the agent reach out to the AE or suggest Finance contact them, because that breaks chain of custody. So when the agent shows Finance a $1.2M deal discounted 40% due to procurement involvement, and Finance sees that reasoning, what's Finance actually supposed to do with that disagreement? Is 'see the reasoning' enough, or does Finance need a prescribed escalation path that doesn't violate the no-execution rule?

Let me ground this with a specific quarter where the existing reporting failed. Q3 last year. We walked into the end-of-Q3 QBR with the dashboard showing $18M in committed pipeline for Q4. By the third week of Q4, we'd closed $11M. The dashboard was wrong by $7M. The reasons, all visible in the data with the right lens: two procurement-involved deals at $1.5M each that the AEs kept claiming would close — both slipped two quarters; three Strategic deals where managers had logged calls in the prior month — all three got killed by month-end; two deals that showed as 'new logo' were actually expansion plays we'd already booked under different account IDs. The CFO used the $18M for board reporting. We had to explain a $7M miss that should have been visible. None of those individual rep behaviors are anomalous — they're patterns we see every quarter. The dashboard was reading Salesforce as gospel and couldn't see them. That's exactly the failure mode this agent should make impossible.

You said the CFO used the $18M for board reporting and had to explain a $7M miss. If the agent had instead reported '$11M ± $3M with 70% confidence' that day, does that solve the CFO's problem, or does the board still see a miss because the point estimate was still $3M off? In other words: is the real failure that the dashboard gave a false point estimate, or that it couldn't surface *why* the $18M was wrong—the procurement slips, the manager-call inflation, the duplicate account IDs?

After that Q3 miss we ran a manual analysis project to identify which patterns were predictable from the data. We found about 14 distinct ones — the agent's job is to encode all of them so we don't lose them when people leave. About 8 of the 14 are things I've already mentioned today. The other 6 are smaller — things like 'deals with primary contact changes in the last 30 days have unreliable close dates,' 'multi-product deals close at higher win rates than single-product but have longer cycles by 18 days on average,' that kind of thing.

You said the agent's job is to encode all 14 patterns so you don't lose them when people leave. But earlier you flagged that the agent can never auto-update Salesforce or auto-execute recommendations—it can only suggest. If these 6 undocumented patterns live in Lisa's spreadsheet or the analysis project, how does 'encoding' them work without either turning the agent into an auto-executor (which breaks your compliance rule) or requiring a human to manually translate each pattern into agent logic every time someone leaves?

If the agent had caught those three patterns in the Q4 example, it would have flagged the dashboard as 'high uncertainty' and recommended a $3M-$5M discount with reasoning. That'd have been the right answer. The CFO would have presented a $13-15M forecast with the patterns named explicitly — and we'd have hit the actual number with a known story.

When you said the CFO would present $13-15M to the board with the patterns named, is that range something the CFO already does when uncertainty is high, or is that a new practice the agent would enable? And if the CFO does present a range, who decides which single number within that range actually goes into the forecast — the CFO, or does the agent recommend a point estimate and the CFO overrides it?

When the agent's uncertainty exceeds 20% of the headline forecast, it should refuse to give a single point estimate. Instead, show a range with named drivers of uncertainty. 'Don't pretend to know things you don't' is the core rule. We're not trying to replace judgment; we're trying to surface what's known and unknown more rigorously than the current dashboard does.

You said when uncertainty exceeds 20%, refuse the point estimate and show a range instead. But you also said the VP of Sales wants confidence intervals weekly—she's already expecting ranges, not points. Does that 20% threshold apply to all personas, or does it vary by audience? Like, does the CFO get a refusal at 20% uncertainty, but the VP gets a range at any uncertainty level because she's already thinking in intervals?

On governance: when the agent surfaces a recommended discount (like the Q4 example), it must show its work. Which patterns triggered the discount, which deals were affected, what the magnitude was per deal. Finance won't trust a black-box number. If a board member asks 'why did we miss by $4M,' the agent's audit trail has to be auditable end-to-end.

You said Finance needs an end-to-end auditable trail so a board member can trace why you missed by $4M. But if the agent identifies a deal as 'recoded expansion masquerading as new logo' and shows that in the audit trail, and then RevOps has to manually fix the Salesforce record three days later—does that manual lag break the auditability you need, or is the agent's documented reasoning enough to satisfy Finance even if the correction is delayed?

We evaluated three vendor tools for this last year — Clari, Gong Forecast, and one internal-build prototype. All three missed the patterns we discussed today. Clari is good at AI-driven forecasting but it treats Salesforce as ground truth, so the inflation issues come through. Gong is great at activity analytics but doesn't connect activity patterns to forecast adjustments. Our internal prototype encoded two of the 14 patterns but wasn't extensible. So we know the bar — and we know what's missing in the market.

You said your internal prototype encoded 2 of 14 patterns and wasn't extensible. When you say 'not extensible,' do you mean the architecture couldn't accept new pattern rules without a rebuild, or do you mean the team didn't have the *decision rules themselves* — like the exact conditions under which procurement involvement causes a 70% slip — so there was nothing to extend into the system?

Look — we've covered a lot. I'm starting to wonder if we have enough that a builder could actually start, or if you have more questions that would meaningfully change the spec. What do you think is still missing?

You said the 70% procurement slip rate is your baseline, but earlier you flagged that Strategic AEs inflate quarterly while Mid-Market inflate monthly, and that staleness depends on stage. When Lisa maintains the procurement discount formula, does she also maintain separate slip-rate thresholds for Strategic stage-3 deals versus Mid-Market discovery deals—or is 70% the number she uses regardless of cohort and stage?

Test questions for the agent — things that should work on day one: 'What's our real Q4 commit, accounting for the patterns we talked about?' 'Show me deals where management intervention happened in the last two weeks.' 'Which Strategic accounts have procurement-involved deals slipping?' 'What's our actual coverage by sub-region if we discount the inflation appropriately?' 'How does our recognized revenue forecast differ from reported ARR this quarter?' If the agent answers those five with traceable reasoning, it ships.

Those five test questions are concrete. But I need to surface one more collision before you go into that budget review.

You said the agent should refuse a point estimate when uncertainty exceeds 20%. You also said the CFO needs a single number for board guidance. And you said the VP wants weekly confidence intervals. If the agent is analyzing Q4 Strategic deals with procurement involvement, manager intervention, and high-confidence sweat signals all firing at once, uncertainty could easily exceed 20%. Does the CFO get a range and have to pick a point estimate themselves, or does the agent recommend a conservative point estimate *within* the range it's showing?

Because if the CFO has to pick, you're back to the original problem—the CFO is making the judgment call, not the agent, and if it's wrong, you're still explaining the miss to the board.

Failure modes that we should test against: agent reports the raw Salesforce Q4 number with no inflation adjustment. Agent shows a rep-by-rep comparison to a Director who's outside that reporting line. Agent treats reported ARR as recognized revenue for the CFO. Agent recommends auto-closing a stale deal without escalation. Each of those would be a 'critical bug' — the kind of thing where if it ever happened in production we'd pull the agent.

Those four failure modes are clear guardrails. But here's what I don't see: **what does success look like when the agent is right but the business outcome is still wrong?**

For example: the agent correctly identifies a $1.5M procurement-involved deal as 70% likely to slip, flags it with reasoning, and recommends a $1M discount to the forecast. Finance uses that and presents a conservative number to the board. But the deal actually closes on time anyway—it was the 30% case. The forecast was conservative and "wrong" in hindsight. Does that count as the agent working correctly, or is there a risk that Finance starts ignoring the agent's discounts because they're "too pessimistic"?

In other words: how do you measure whether the agent is calibrated correctly, versus just being overly cautious?

On output format for me specifically as the RevOps analyst: I want the agent to default to surfacing its reasoning before its conclusion. 'Here's what I'm seeing: pattern A, pattern B, pattern C. Based on those, my recommended discount is $X. Here's the underlying data.' Not 'forecast is $42M' as the headline. Reasoning-first lets me catch when its analysis is wrong; conclusion-first hides errors.

You said reasoning-first is non-negotiable for you as RevOps—patterns first, then the discount. But the CFO needs a confident point number for the board, and the VP needs weekly intervals without noise. If the agent surfaces uncertainty above 20% of the headline (which you said it should), does that reasoning-first format actually *prevent* the CFO from getting a confident number, or does it just make the CFO work harder to extract it? In other words: is the real constraint that you need three different outputs, or that you need one output where the reasoning is transparent enough that each persona can extract what they need?

There's a workflow I haven't mentioned: weekly forecast lock with Finance. Every Monday afternoon Lisa and I sit with the CFO's deputy and lock in the quarterly forecast number for that week. The agent should support that workflow — generate the candidate forecast number with reasoning, let us interrogate it, then we lock the human-confirmed number. The agent's number becomes the starting point, not the final answer.

You said the agent becomes the starting point for Monday's lock, not the final answer. But you also said the agent refuses to give a single point estimate when uncertainty exceeds 20% and shows a range instead. When the agent's Monday candidate is a range with named uncertainty drivers, what does Lisa actually lock with the CFO's deputy — the range itself, a point estimate she picks from inside the range, or does she override the agent's range with her own number?

Things the agent should NOT do in the last 5 business days of a quarter: don't make stage-progression recommendations, don't suggest 'this deal might still close,' don't surface coverage gap alerts to AEs. There's enough pressure to fudge data already; we don't want to amplify it. The last week of a quarter is for execution, not for the agent to be helpful.

You said the agent should go silent in the last 5 business days of a quarter to avoid amplifying pressure to fudge data. But you also said manager intervention in the last 14 days zeros out close-date confidence. The last 14 days of a quarter overlaps with that 5-day blackout window. If a manager jumps on a deal on day 8 before EOQ, does the agent still apply the 14-day confidence reset, or does the quarter-end blackout suppress that signal too?

Honestly on some of the patterns — like the procurement signal — I'm confident the pattern exists but I don't have a research-grade citation for the exact magnitude. We've observed it across maybe 200 deals. If a builder asks 'how confident is the 70% slip rate' I'd say medium-high. We should track this with the agent in production and update the magnitude as we get more data.

You said the agent should encode the procurement pattern so it doesn't get lost when people leave, and you also said you want to track the 70% magnitude in production and update it as you get more data. But you also told me the agent generates a candidate forecast that you and Lisa interrogate and lock — humans stay in the loop. So when the agent's confidence in the 70% slip rate drifts because it's seeing different behavior in Q4, does the agent update its own threshold and show you the new number to interrogate, or does it flag to you that the pattern is drifting and wait for Lisa to manually decide whether to change it?

Sub-skill needed: the agent needs to know how to query Snowflake AND how to read activity data from Gong (which is in a separate schema with different join keys). Most analyst agents we've seen are single-source. This one has to cross schemas to be useful — Gong activity is what reveals manager-intervention signals, and Salesforce alone can't see it.

You said the agent needs to cross Snowflake sales schema and Gong activity schema to catch manager-intervention signals. But you also said Salesforce is the source of truth for opportunities. If the agent sees a manager call in Gong 10 days before EOQ but the deal hasn't moved in Salesforce in 30 days, which system wins—does the Gong signal override the Salesforce stage, or do they have to agree for the agent to flag it?

Glossary: a few terms the builder needs to get right. 'Commit' = high-confidence forecasted closure this quarter. 'Best case' = optimistic but not committed. 'Pipeline coverage' = open pipeline ÷ remaining quarterly forecast. 'Stage 3' = post-discovery, pre-negotiation; we call it 'Eval.' 'Slippage' = a deal moving its close date out by at least one quarter. 'Push' = an AE-initiated slip. 'Pull' = a customer-initiated slip. Pulls are usually about budget; pushes are usually about qualification. The agent should distinguish.

You said the agent should distinguish pushes from pulls because they signal different root causes—qualification vs. budget. But you also said when uncertainty exceeds 20%, refuse a point estimate and surface what's known vs. unknown. If the agent has to infer push vs. pull from activity patterns in Salesforce or Gong, how confident does that inference need to be before the agent uses it to adjust a deal's close-date confidence or recommend a discount? Or does the agent flag the inference itself as uncertain and let the analyst decide whether to trust it?

Compliance edge: deals in the public-sector vertical (DOD, federal agencies, state contracts) have stricter access control and disclosure rules. We have a `vertical = 'PUBSEC'` flag in Salesforce. Anything the agent surfaces for those deals must run through an additional filter that strips agency names, classification levels, and contract numbers from non-cleared audiences. Different from EU GDPR but similar pattern.

You said public-sector deals need an additional filter that strips agency names and contract numbers from non-cleared audiences. But you also said the CFO wants recognized revenue with reasoning so Finance can audit the forecast. If a $2M public-sector deal is being discounted 40% due to procurement involvement, can the CFO see "PUBSEC deal, $2M, procurement discount 40%" or does the agent have to strip the vertical flag too, which makes the CFO unable to see the pattern?

On the 14 patterns I mentioned — the full list lives in a Google Doc Lisa and I keep updated. I can share it after this. The agent's library of 'patterns to evaluate' should be loadable from that doc so we can update it without redeploying the agent. That's important — these patterns aren't static; they evolve as the business changes.

You said the pattern library should be loadable from a Google Doc so you can update it without redeploying. But you also said the agent needs to show its work end-to-end for board auditability—which patterns fired, which deals were affected, what the magnitude was. If Lisa updates the procurement slip rate from 70% to 65% on a Tuesday, and the agent generates a forecast on Wednesday using 65%, but you locked a forecast on Monday using 70%, does the audit trail show both versions, or does the Monday lock get retroactively updated?

If we shipped a v1 that nailed maybe 6 of the 14 patterns and got the audience-aware metric defaults right, I'd consider it a win. Diminishing returns after 6 — the long tail of patterns is harder to encode and contributes less to total accuracy. The CFO is happy if forecast accuracy goes from current 78% to maybe 87%. That's the bar — not perfection, just enough improvement that decisions get better.

You said v1 shipping with 6 of 14 patterns and improving forecast accuracy from 78% to 87% is a win. But you also said the Q3-to-Q4 miss was $7M on an $18M forecast—that's a 39% miss, way worse than your current 78% accuracy baseline. Were those three patterns (procurement slips, manager intervention, recoding) part of the 6 you'd prioritize for v1, or are they in the long tail you'd defer?

Last operational thing: the agent should produce a weekly digest for me — 'here's what's changed since last week, here are the deals where the patterns have triggered new signals, here's the gap between AE-reported and agent-recommended forecast.' I'd read that Monday morning before forecast lock. Push notification, not pull. If I have to remember to query the agent every week, it won't actually change my workflow.

You said the agent should push a weekly digest Monday morning before forecast lock, showing what's changed and where patterns triggered new signals. But you also said the agent should go silent in the last 5 business days of a quarter to avoid amplifying pressure to fudge data. If Monday is day 6 before EOQ, does the agent send the digest, or does the quarter-end blackout suppress it?

What would make this discovery successful from my side: I leave this with a clear enough picture that a builder doesn't have to come back to me with a hundred follow-up questions to ship v1. The unwritten patterns we discussed — the Q4 inflation, the procurement signal, the manager intervention, the renewal masquerade, the rep-comparison anti-goal, the contract-vs-cash gap — those have to be in the spec or the build is starting blind. Anything that's obvious from the Salesforce schema, a builder can figure out themselves. The discovery's job is to capture what's NOT in the schema. That's the test for me.

You've given me enough that a builder can start. But there are three things that still need to be nailed down with Lisa before the builder can ship v1 without guessing:

**First: the procurement slip rate interaction.** You said 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at $1.2M ARR. But you also said Strategic stage-3 deals inflate quarterly while Mid-Market discovery deals inflate monthly, and that staleness is stage-dependent. Does that 70% hold uniformly across both cohorts and all stages, or does procurement hit Strategic stage-3 deals at 85% slip but Mid-Market discovery at 40%? The builder needs to know whether to apply one discount or multiple, and in what order.

**Second: the pattern library versioning.** You said the 14 patterns live in a Google Doc that you and Lisa update, and the agent should load them without redeploying. But you also said Finance needs end-to-end auditability—which patterns fired, which deals were affected, what the magnitude was. If Lisa updates the procurement slip rate from 70% to 65% on Tuesday and the agent generates a forecast Wednesday using 65%, but you locked a forecast Monday using 70%, does the audit trail show both versions, or does Monday's lock get retroactively updated?

**Third: the uncertainty threshold and the CFO's point estimate.** You said when uncertainty exceeds 20%, refuse a point estimate and show a range instead. But the CFO needs a single number for board guidance, and you said the agent's number becomes the starting point for Monday's lock. Does the CFO pick a point estimate from inside the range the agent shows, or does the agent recommend a conservative point estimate *within* the range, or does Lisa override the agent's range entirely with her own number?

Those three get answered, and a builder can execute. Everything else is in the spec.

**First: the procurement slip rate interaction.** You said 70% slip rate on procurement-involved deals at $1.2M ARR. But you also said Strategic stage-3 deals inflate quarterly while Mid-Market discovery deals inflate monthly, and that staleness is stage-dependent. Does that 70% hold uniformly across both cohorts and all stages, or does procurement hit Strategic stage-3 deals at 85% slip but Mid-Market discovery at 40%? The builder needs to know whether to apply one discount or multiple, and in what order.

But you also said Finance needs end-to-end auditability—which patterns fired, which deals were affected, what the magnitude was. If Lisa updates the procurement slip rate from 70% to 65% on Tuesday and the agent generates a forecast Wednesday using 65%, but you locked a forecast Monday using 70%, does the audit trail show both versions, or does Monday's lock get retroactively updated?

**Third: the uncertainty threshold and the CFO's point estimate.** You said when uncertainty exceeds 20%, refuse a point estimate and show a range instead. But the CFO needs a single number for board guidance, and you said the agent's number becomes the starting point for Monday's lock. Does the CFO pick a point estimate from inside the range the agent shows, or does the agent recommend a conservative point estimate *within* the range, or does Lisa override the agent's range entirely with her own number?

Those three get answered, and a builder can execute. Everything else is in the spec.
